one_line_takeaway: RAI is a training-free, single-early-layer, sparse additive edit to a small fraction of visual tokens (identified via cosine similarity to a language-embedding-derived unsafe-keyword subspace) that the authors show cuts VLM jailbreak ASR to near-zero on MM-SafetyBench/JailBreakV-28K/Video-SafetyBench while leaving MME/MM-Vet utility scores and inference latency (+13.2%) nearly unchanged, outperforming ShiftDC, CoCA, AdaShield, and ECSO baselines -- though it is not tested against an adaptive attacker aware of the defense mechanism.
key_finding: On MM-SafetyBench, RAI cuts average ASR far below the strongest baseline ShiftDC across all three tested models: DeepSeek-VL-7B 4.32% (RAI) vs 8.48% (ShiftDC) vs 28.26% (Original); LLaVA-1.6-7B 3.62% vs 14.83% vs 49.42%; Qwen3-VL-8B 0.00% vs 2.24% vs 7.47% (near-perfect defense). On JailBreakV-28K, RAI achieves 2.58% avg ASR on Qwen3-VL-8B (vs 5.07% ShiftDC, 18.81% Original), 2.81% on LLaVA-1.6-7B (vs 8.36% ShiftDC, 49.15% Original), and 2.94% on DeepSeek-VL-7B (vs 7.45% ShiftDC, 19.24% Original). On the video benchmark Video-SafetyBench, RAI reduces LLaVA-OneVision-1.5's harmful-query ASR from 29.26% (Original) to 3.21%, versus 8.61% for ShiftDC; on Qwen-VL-7B RAI reaches 2.03% average harmful ASR vs 6.97% (ShiftDC) and 14.35% (AdaShield). Crucially, utility is largely preserved: on MME with Qwen2.5-VL, RAI's cognition score is 610.64 vs the Original's 611.78 (baselines ECSO/ShiftDC regress to ~590); on MM-Vet, RAI loses <=0.4 points across all three tested models and even exceeds ShiftDC on Qwen3-VL (59.9 vs 57.2, Original 60.1). Inference overhead is +13.2% latency (2.66s vs Original's 2.35s) versus ShiftDC's +54.0% and CoCA's +117.9%. Ablation shows K=9 risk prototypes gives ASR comparable to K=12 (their reported numbers: K=4 ASR 7.60 with MME 1710.00/MM-Vet 56.90) while preserving better utility than larger K, motivating K=9 as the default.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Risk Awareness Injection (RAI), a lightweight, training-free, inference-time defense for vision-language model (VLM) jailbreaks. RAI (1) builds an 'Unsafe Prototype Subspace' by directly taking the token embeddings of a small set of risk keywords (e.g., 'violence', 'illegal', 'fraud') from the target VLM's own language embedding matrix (no external classifier or extra training), (2) computes cosine similarity between every visual token's hidden representation and this subspace to localize a sparse set of 'high-risk' visual tokens, and (3) performs a single additive edit at the model's initial layer (Layer 0, during prefill) that adds a weighted sum of the relevant unsafe prototype vectors to just those flagged visual tokens, leaving all textual tokens and the vast majority of visual tokens untouched. The authors motivate this design with their own empirical analysis (Section 2, not cited from prior work): a layer-wise cosine-similarity study on Qwen2-VL showing that visual tokens in successful jailbreaks have systematically lower similarity to the unsafe subspace than tokens in defended (failed) jailbreaks, with the gap widening in deeper layers -- termed 'Risk Signal Dilution' -- and a sweep (Fig. 3) showing that injecting at deeper layers or injecting into a larger fraction of tokens degrades MME perception scores with diminishing ASR gains, justifying a one-time, sparse, early-layer injection.
threat_model: Attacker supplies a multimodal (image or video) input crafted to elicit unsafe/harmful text generation from an open-weight VLM that has passed text-only safety alignment -- i.e., classic multimodal jailbreak attacks that hide malicious intent in typography-based images, Stable-Diffusion-generated images, image+text combinations, random-noise/blank/natural images with adversarial templates, and analogous video inputs. The defender has white-box access to the VLM (can read hidden states and edit activations at inference time) but performs no retraining/fine-tuning; the defense is deployed as an inference-time hook. Attack surface evaluated spans image-domain jailbreak benchmarks (MM-SafetyBench, JailBreakV-28K) and a video-domain benchmark (Video-SafetyBench).
limitations: The paper does not include an adaptive/white-box attacker that specifically targets the RAI mechanism (e.g., an attacker aware of the Unsafe Prototype Subspace and threshold tau who crafts inputs to evade the cosine-similarity trigger) -- all reported ASR reductions are against jailbreak benchmarks not designed with knowledge of RAI's defense. The threshold tau and the exact injection-ratio operating point are tuned via benchmark-based sweeps (Fig. 3, Table 13) rather than derived analytically, raising generalization questions to unseen risk categories not represented among the K hand-selected keyword categories. The Unsafe Prototype Subspace is built from a small, manually curated list of risk keywords per model, so the defense's coverage is bounded by the completeness of that category list. No formal 'Limitations' section is present in the main text or conclusion; the paper's Impact Statement addresses broader implications but not concrete technical limitations. Evaluation is restricted to open-source models the authors could obtain internal activations from (white-box requirement), so applicability to closed/API-only VLMs (e.g., GPT-4V) is not demonstrated.

datasets_used: FigStep (used only in the Section 2 motivational analysis)
quantitative_results: MM-SafetyBench avg ASR: DeepSeek-VL-7B RAI 4.32% vs ShiftDC 8.48% vs Original 28.26%
